explanation:
Applied physics solves everyday problems: from free fall to household electricity. Formulas are validated by centuries of experiments.

## How It's Calculated

Angular momentum measures the rotational motion of a body. It's calculated by multiplying the moment of inertia I (resistance to rotational acceleration) by the angular velocity ω (rotational speed in radians per second).

This calculator applies the formula to your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting when values seem unusual.

## Key Points

Angular momentum is conserved in closed systems without external torque. When an ice skater pulls in their arms during a spin, their moment of inertia decreases, so their angular velocity increases to keep L constant.

This calculation is for reference. For critical decisions (medical, financial, legal), consult a professional. Data updated through 2026 and periodically reviewed.

faq.a: Angular momentum (L) is a measure of how much rotational motion an object has. It's calculated as the product of moment of inertia I and angular velocity ω.
In systems with no external torque, angular momentum remains constant. This is why a spinning figure skater speeds up when pulling in their arms—moment of inertia decreases while L stays constant.

faq.q: What's the difference between linear and angular momentum?
faq.a: Linear momentum (p = mv) describes straight-line motion. Angular momentum (L = I·ω) describes rotational motion. They follow analogous principles in their respective domains.

faq.q: How do I calculate moment of inertia for common shapes?
faq.a: Common formulas: thin ring = mr², disk = ½mr², sphere = ⅖mr², rod = ⅓ML². Choose the formula that matches your object's geometry.
Yes. Earth has L ≈ 7.07×10³³ kg·m²/s from its rotation. Planetary angular momentum significantly affects solar system dynamics.
